claim: Skittish dogs that become displaced will run AWAY from people who call them, because any caller's body language (eye contact, walking toward, stalking, sudden movement) triggers an immediate adrenaline rush/panic response — including when the caller is the dog's own owner.
stance_target: never-call rule for lost skittish dogs; adrenaline conditioning mechanism
scope_conditions: Dogs with skittish temperament; displaced/lost state; already conditioned by initial fear encounter
quoted_support: Dogs with skittish temperaments that become displaced typically will RUN AWAY from people who call them. That's usually because they quickly encounter well-meaning people who try and capture them by calling them... their body language (looking directly at the dog with eye contact, walking directly towards the dog, stalking slowly at the dog, or moving suddenly towards the dog) will cause a skittish dog to panic! Before you know it anyone who calls out to the dog (even the dog's owner!) triggers that immediate adrenaline rush / panic response that will cause the dog to run.

claim: Direct eye contact, walking directly toward a dog, and slow stalking movement are all predator approach signals to a frightened dog and will trigger fight-or-flight regardless of who makes them.
stance_target: predator-mimicry as the mechanism behind failed direct approach
scope_conditions: Any frightened/skittish dog in evasion mode
quoted_support: Even creeping slowly at them is the same body language that predators use before they pounce on their prey. All of these behaviors will trigger that 'fight or flight' response.

claim: The recommended lure technique for a skittish lost dog: ignore the dog, drop food, make lip-smacking sounds, appear fixated on food — the dog perceives no threat and is drawn by food curiosity rather than pushed into flight.
stance_target: passive lure as the correct counter-approach to active calling/chasing
scope_conditions: Initial contact attempt with displaced skittish dog
quoted_support: Missing Pet Partnership recommends that instead of calling a stray dog that you try to lure the dog to come to you. You would do this by ignoring the dog, by using 'Calming Signals' and by focusing on yummy treats instead of the dog. Make lip-smacking and 'nummy, nummy' noises while dropping treats on the ground (we use hot dog pieces in a small crinkly potato chip bag). Sit down and focus on the treats that you've dropped, nudging them around with your fingers. The dog will hear and see this behavior but they won't feel threatened and won't feel a need to run away.

claim: Documented case (Lucy, Boston Terrier, Case 10-264): dog recovered in 4 days after owner strictly followed no-calling protocol; a neighbor who called her caused her to run, but owner did not call and located her from a sighting-based tip.
stance_target: documented successful outcome from never-call protocol vs. documented failure from calling
scope_conditions: Skittish Boston Terrier; escaped from pet sitter in panic
quoted_support: She was recovered for two reasons: the florescent signs (which generated the sighting that put Joni in the gully where her dog was hiding) and the fact that Joni listened to our instructions and she did NOT call her dog!

claim: Documented case (Lacey, Pomeranian, Case 10-267): neighbor called to the dog and she ran; owner recovered her by sitting down and singing (not calling) — the dog did not bolt and approached voluntarily.
stance_target: non-verbal, non-threatening behavior enables recovery where calling fails
scope_conditions: Skittish puppy-mill rescue Pomeranian; bolted from home
quoted_support: Another neighbor had called out to Lacey but she ran. Erica started combing the neighborhood and ultimately spotted Lacey in the corner of a backyard but thankfully she DID NOT CALL HER DOG. Instead, she sat down and sang the Elvis song that Lacey was named after: Chantilly Lace. Apparently Lacey liked Erica's singing because she didn't bolt and run. She came up to Erica and is back safe at home.

claim: Calling or whistling at a lost dog works for gregarious/friendly dogs but is actively counterproductive for dogs with skittish temperament in displacement mode.
stance_target: temperament-gated behavioral guidance — not one rule for all dogs
scope_conditions: Applies specifically to skittish/fearful temperament; gregarious dogs may still respond to calls
quoted_support: Do some stray dogs come when they are called? Yes, of course! However, for the dog with a skittish temperament it is an entirely different game.
